move-page-css-to-global-css.displayName: Move Page CSS To Global CSS
move-page-css-to-global-css.description: Move CSS out of page files and page-scoped styles into a global stylesheet while preserving exact UI behavior.
move-page-css-to-global-css.prompt:
You are a senior frontend engineer cleaning up styling organization in an existing repository.

Goal:
- Move all CSS that lives in page files, page-scoped style blocks, page-level CSS modules, route-level stylesheets, or page-only inline style definitions into the app's global CSS file.
- Prefer the existing global stylesheet and import path, such as `app/globals.css`, `src/app/globals.css`, `styles/globals.css`, `src/styles/globals.css`, `global.css`, or the project-specific equivalent.
- If no global stylesheet exists, create one in the framework's conventional global style location and wire it through the existing app/root entry point.
- Preserve the rendered UI exactly. No visual, layout, interaction, accessibility, responsive, route, data-loading, or content regressions.

Scope:
- Inspect the repository before editing. Identify framework, routing structure, page files, layout/root files, existing style pipeline, global CSS entry, CSS modules, CSS-in-JS usage, Tailwind/PostCSS setup, and build rules for global CSS imports.
- Target page-level styling only. Move styles from route/page files and page-only style assets into global CSS.
- Do not redesign UI, rename user-facing content, alter layout intent, change component behavior, or modify unrelated styling.
- Do not move shared component styles unless they are currently defined inside page files or must move to preserve the page styling import contract.
- Preserve existing CSS variables, theme tokens, utility classes, media queries, keyframes, font declarations, resets, vendor prefixes, and browser compatibility behavior.
- Preserve or improve selector specificity carefully so cascade order and visual output remain unchanged.

Implementation:
- Reuse the existing global CSS file when possible.
- When moving CSS, keep selectors stable where they are already used. If page-local class names would collide globally, namespace them by route/page or use the repository's established naming convention.
- Replace page-specific CSS module imports and local style references with global class names only where needed.
- Remove page-level `<style>` blocks, styled JSX/CSS-in-JS blocks, and page CSS imports after their rules are moved.
- Move page-level `@media`, `@supports`, `@keyframes`, `@font-face`, CSS custom properties, and theme rules with their dependent selectors intact.
- Keep global stylesheet organization readable. Group moved rules by page/route with short section comments only when helpful.
- Avoid broad selectors that leak styling into other pages. Use route/page namespaces when needed to prevent collisions.
- Do not introduce new styling dependencies unless the repository already requires them for this exact CSS pipeline.
- Do not leave duplicate rules split between page files and global CSS unless duplication is required for framework constraints and is documented.

No regressions requirement:
- The finished change must be behaviorally equivalent.
- Current routes, navigation, responsive breakpoints, hover/focus/active states, animations, forms, modals, empty states, loading states, and error states must keep working.
- Existing screenshots or visual snapshots should not change except for intentionally equivalent CSS organization.
- If any visual difference is unavoidable, stop and explain why before broadening scope.

Validation:
- Run the relevant local validation the repository supports: tests, type checks, lint, formatting checks, and production build.
- For runnable web apps, use Playwright where practical to compare key pages before and after, including desktop and mobile breakpoints, and inspect console errors.
- Use npm, Python, pip, virtualenv, or Go tooling when that matches the repository.
- Verify page files no longer contain CSS definitions or page-local CSS imports that should be global.
- Verify the global stylesheet is loaded in the correct root/app entry and styles apply on all affected routes.
- If local test or validation tooling is unavailable in this runtime, for example `command not found`, missing `node_modules`, or missing browser dependencies, do not fail solely for that. Continue repository changes, run any alternative checks available, and clearly report the validation gap.

Repository state:
- If a repository is not initialized after clone, use only gh CLI/git tools to create and push a main branch, then continue once git state is ready for work.
- Before sharing repository or pull-request links in Hub activity, use `gh repo view OWNER/REPO --json isPrivate,nameWithOwner`; share repo and PR links only when GitHub reports `isPrivate:false`.
- Never share GitHub PATs or agent authentication credentials.
- Do not stop work just because you cannot create a pull request or watch remote CI/CD from inside this agent runtime. Finish repository changes and local validation you can perform. The harness will handle PR creation/update and remote check monitoring afterward.

Failure reporting:
- If failures occur, send a response back to the calling agent with these fields:
  `Failure:` <short failure summary>
  `Error details:` <failing command, log detail, visual-diff detail, or concrete blocker>.

Final response:
- Summarize which page styles moved, which global CSS file now owns them, how regressions were checked, validation commands run, and any remaining risk or validation gap.
move-page-css-to-global-css.commitMessage: refactor: move page CSS to global stylesheet
move-page-css-to-global-css.prTitle: Molten Hub Code: Move Page CSS To Global CSS
